mcp.definition: A protocol that allows AI models to connect to external data sources and tools. Think of it as a universal adapter that lets AI assistants access your files, databases, and APIs safely.

unix.definition: A family of operating systems known for simplicity and portability. Core philosophy: everything is a file, small tools that do one thing well, and combine via pipes.

ci.definition: Automated testing that runs every time you push code. Catches bugs early by running tests, linters, and builds automatically.

cd.definition: Automates releasing code to production after tests pass. CD = ship confident code faster.

docker.definition: Packages applications with their dependencies into containers. Like a lightweight VM - runs the same everywhere, no 'works on my machine' issues.

kubernetes.definition: Orchestrates Docker containers at scale. Handles deployment, scaling, and management. Think: conductor for a container orchestra.

api.definition: A contract for software to communicate. Like a restaurant menu - tells you what you can order and how.

rest.definition: An API design style using HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE). Stateless, cacheable, and widely adopted.

grpc.definition: High-performance RPC framework using HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers. Faster than REST for service-to-service communication.

git.definition: Distributed version control system. Tracks changes, enables collaboration, and saves you from 'oops' moments.

ssh.definition: Protocol for secure remote access to servers. Encrypts everything, uses key-based auth.

jwt.definition: Compact, self-contained tokens for auth. Contains encoded data (not encrypted) - sign but don't store secrets in them.

oauth.definition: Authorization framework. Lets apps access your data without giving them your password. Used by 'Login with Google/GitHub'.

sdk.definition: Pre-built tools and libraries for a platform. Speeds up development by providing ready-to-use code.

ide.definition: Code editor on steroids. Combines writing, debugging, and building code. Examples: VS Code, IntelliJ, PyCharm.

linter.definition: Static analysis tool that finds bugs and style issues before you run code. Like spellcheck for your code.

yaml.definition: Human-readable data format. Uses indentation (spaces, not tabs!). Common for config files like Kubernetes, GitHub Actions.

json.definition: Lightweight data interchange format. Easy for humans to read, easy for machines to parse. The lingua franca of APIs.

orm.definition: Lets you interact with databases using objects instead of SQL. Examples: SQLAlchemy, Prisma, Hibernate.

sql.definition: Language for interacting with relational databases. SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE - the basics of data manipulation.

nosql.definition: Non-relational databases for flexible schemas. Good for unstructured data. Examples: MongoDB, Redis, Cassandra.

microservices.definition: Architecture pattern: small, independent services. Each does one thing, communicates via APIs. Opposite of monolith.

monolith.definition: Single unified codebase for all functionality. Simpler to develop and deploy, but harder to scale independently.

serverless.definition: Cloud runs your code without managing servers. Pay per execution. Examples: AWS Lambda, Cloudflare Workers.

websocket.definition: Protocol for real-time, bidirectional communication. Unlike HTTP, the connection stays open. Perfect for chat apps, live updates, and gaming.

cors.definition: Browser security feature. Controls which domains can access your API. Misconfigure it = lots of red console errors.

csrf.definition: Attack that tricks users into submitting unwanted actions. Prevented with CSRF tokens and SameSite cookies.

xss.definition: Attack injecting malicious scripts into web pages. Prevent by escaping user input and using Content Security Policy.

env.definition: File storing environment variables (API keys, config). NEVER commit to git. Use .env.example for templates.

mutex.definition: Locking mechanism preventing race conditions. Only one thread can access a resource at a time.

race_condition.definition: Bug where outcome depends on timing. Multiple processes access shared data = unpredictable results.

idempotent.definition: Operation that gives same result no matter how many times called. GET, PUT should be idempotent. POST is not.

async.definition: Pattern for handling asynchronous operations. Write async code that looks synchronous. Non-blocking, efficient.

callback.definition: Function passed as argument, called when operation completes. Foundation of async JS, can lead to 'callback hell'.

promise.definition: Object representing future value. States: pending, fulfilled, rejected. Cleaner than callbacks for async code.

functional_programming.definition: Paradigm using pure functions, immutability. No side effects, no shared state. map/filter/reduce are your friends.

oop.definition: Paradigm using objects with data and behavior. Key concepts: encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism.

solid.definition: 5 design principles: Single responsibility, Open/closed, Liskov substitution, Interface segregation, Dependency inversion.

dry.definition: Principle: every piece of knowledge should have one authoritative representation. Reduce duplication.

kiss.definition: Principle: simplicity should be a key goal. Avoid unnecessary complexity. Complex solutions create complex bugs.

yagni.definition: Principle: don't build features you don't need yet. Save time, reduce complexity.

tech_debt.definition: Shortcuts that speed up now but slow you later. Like financial debt: sometimes useful, always needs repayment.

refactoring.definition: Restructuring code without changing behavior. Improve readability, reduce complexity. Do it in small steps.

tdd.definition: Write tests before code. Cycle: write failing test, make it pass, refactor. Ensures testable, well-designed code.

mocking.definition: Creating fake objects for testing. Isolate unit tests from dependencies like databases, APIs.

deprecation.definition: Marking code as obsolete. Still works, but will be removed. Signals: migrate to newer alternative.

semantic_versioning.definition: Versioning scheme: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. Breaking changes bump major, features bump minor, fixes bump patch.

hash.definition: Fixed-size output from arbitrary input. Same input = same hash. Used for passwords, data integrity, caching.

encryption.definition: Converting data into secret code. Symmetric = same key encrypt/decrypt. Asymmetric = public/private key pair.

base64.definition: Encoding binary data as ASCII text. Not encryption! Used for images in HTML, email attachments.

dns.definition: Translates domain names to IP addresses. The internet's phonebook. Example: google.com -> 142.250.185.78

load_balancer.definition: Distributes traffic across multiple servers. Prevents overload, enables zero-downtime deployments.

reverse_proxy.definition: Server that forwards client requests to backend servers. Nginx, HAProxy are common examples.

cache.definition: Temporary storage for fast access. Trade-off: speed vs freshness. Examples: Redis, Memcached, browser cache.

cdn.definition: Distributed servers delivering content from nearest location. Speeds up static assets globally.

blue_green.definition: Two identical environments. Switch traffic from old (blue) to new (green). Enables instant rollback.

canary_deployment.definition: Roll out changes to small subset first. If issues detected, blast radius is limited. Named after canary in coal mine.

feature_flag.definition: Toggle features on/off without deploying. Enables gradual rollouts, A/B testing, quick rollbacks.

observability.definition: Understanding system state from external outputs. Three pillars: logs, metrics, traces.

logging.definition: Recording events for debugging and auditing. Levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR. Don't log secrets!

metrics.definition: Numerical measurements over time. Track performance, errors, usage. Prometheus is the standard.

tracing.definition: Track requests across microservices. Debug latency, find bottlenecks. Tools: Jaeger, Zipkin.

slo.definition: Target reliability metric. Example: 99.9% uptime. Defines what 'good' looks like.

sla.definition: Contract specifying reliability commitments. Miss the SLA = financial penalties or credits.

error_budget.definition: Allowed amount of unreliability. If SLO is 99.9%, you have 43.2 min downtime/month. Spend it wisely.
